# Team Specification Format

Team specs are saved as YAML files at user-specified paths. This is the canonical format.

## Schema

```yaml
# Team Specification
# Generated by /assemble-team
# Date: YYYY-MM-DD

meta:
  name: "descriptive-team-name"
  goal: "One-sentence description of what this team accomplishes"
  mode: manual | guided | automatic  # how the spec was generated
  rationale: |
    Why this team structure was chosen over alternatives.
    What single-agent alternative was considered and why it was rejected.

workflow:
  type: linear | branching | parallel | loop
  description: "How agents interact and in what order"
  phases:
    - name: "phase-name"
      agent: "agent-id"
      gate: "exit criteria before advancing (optional)"

agents:
  - id: "agent-id"
    role: "one-line description of what this agent does"
    justification: "why this agent exists as a separate entity"
    single_agent_test: "what would be lost if this agent's job were folded into another"

    input:
      description: "what this agent receives"
      schema:  # optional, for structured inputs
        field_name: "type — description"

    output:
      description: "what this agent must produce"
      schema:  # optional, for structured outputs
        field_name: "type — description"

    tools:
      allowed:
        - "tool_name — brief description of allowed use"
      denied:
        - "tool_name — why this agent must not have access"

    context:
      receives:
        - "what information is passed to this agent"
      excluded:
        - "what is deliberately withheld and why"

    constraints:
      - "hard rules this agent must follow"

    prompt: |
      The full agent prompt, following the constraint-based template:
      You [verb]. You do not [boundary].
      INPUT: ...
      PROCESS: ...
      OUTPUT: ...
      TOOLS: ...
      CONSTRAINTS: ...

    # Environment-specific adaptations (populated based on target)
    adapters:
      claude_code:  # Claude Code sub-agent
        agent_file: "path to .claude/agents/name.md"
        allowed_tools: ["list", "of", "tools"]
        model: "sonnet | opus | haiku"
      nanoclaw:  # NanoClaw container agent
        container_skill: "path in container/skills/"
        mcp_servers: ["list of MCP servers to mount"]
      generic:  # Environment-agnostic
        description: "how to implement in any agent framework"

review:
  type: generator_critic | reflection | adversarial_debate | deterministic | hybrid
  description: "how quality is verified"
  rounds: 2  # max review iterations (research says 2-3 optimal, 5 ceiling)
  criteria:
    - "specific, checkable criterion"
  deterministic_checks:
    - "linter/test/validator that runs before LLM review"

cost_estimate:
  agents: 3
  estimated_turns: "total LLM calls for one run of the workflow"
  token_multiplier: "roughly Nx vs single-agent approach"
  justification: "why the overhead is worth it for this task"
```

## Adapter Notes

### Claude Code Adapter
- Agents become `.claude/agents/*.md` files with YAML frontmatter
- `allowed-tools` in frontmatter controls tool access
- Model selection via frontmatter
- Invoked with `/agents/name` or via the Agent tool

### NanoClaw Adapter
- Agents become container skills in `container/skills/`
- Tool access controlled via `allowed-tools` frontmatter and MCP server mounting
- Can leverage task slots for parallel agent execution

### Generic Adapter
- Framework-agnostic description
- Can be adapted to Claude Agent SDK, LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, etc.
- Focus on the prompt and constraints, not the framework-specific wiring
